|Largest loss  = {{fb|AUS}} [[Australia 31-0 American Samoa|31–0]] {{fb-rt|ASA}}<br>([[Coffs Harbour]], [[Australia]]; April 11, 2001)<br><small>(World record for biggest defeat in senior international matches)</small>
|Regional name = [[OFC Nations Cup]]
|2ndRegional cup apps = 6
|2ndRegional name = [[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]

|3rdRegional name = [[Polynesia Cup]]
|3rdRegional cup first = [[1994 Polynesia Cup|1994]]

|3rdRegional cup apps = 3
}}

The '''American Samoa men's national football team''' ({{langx|sm|Au soka Amerika Sāmoa}}) represents [[American Samoa]] in men's international [[association football]] and is controlled by the [[Football Federation American Samoa]], the governing body of the sport in the territory. American Samoa's home ground is the [[Pago Park Soccer Stadium]] in [[Pago Pago]].

==History==

=== Early history ===
In 1983, [[American Samoa]] entered a football team in the [[1983 South Pacific Games|South Pacific Games]] for the first time. The territory's official first match took place in [[Apia]], [[Samoa|Western Samoa]] on August 20, 1983, and ended in a 3–1 defeat to [[Samoa national football team|Western Samoa]]. Two days later, the team recorded their first win with a 3–0 victory against [[Wallis and Futuna national football team|Wallis & Futuna]] however, they were eliminated from the competition at the group stage following a 3–2 loss to [[Tonga national football team|Tonga]] on August 24.<ref name="SPG1983">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac83.html |title=South Pacific Games 1983 (Western Samoa) |date=October 17, 2016 |first1=Mark |last1=Cruickshank |first2=Neil |last2=Morrison |first3=Pieter |last3=Veroeveren |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=August 3, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220803013601/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac83.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Elo">{{cite web |url=https://www.eloratings.net/Eastern_Samoa |title=World Football Elo Ratings: Eastern Samoa |publisher=World Football Elo Ratings |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=October 28, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211028171114/https://www.eloratings.net/Eastern_Samoa |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="RSSSF">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/amsamoa-intres.html |title=American Samoa – List of international matches |date=November 23, 2006 |first=Barrie |last=Courtney |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=July 24, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220724164808/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/amsamoa-intres.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

The following year, the [[Football Federation American Samoa|American Samoa Football Association]] (ASFA) – now known as Football Federation American Samoa (FFAS) – was founded and took over responsibility for organizing the territory's national team.<ref name="FIFA">{{cite web |url=https://www.fifa.com/associations/association/asa/about |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190903111343/https://www.fifa.com/associations/association/asa/about |url-status=dead |archive-date=September 3, 2019 |title=Member Association – American Samoa |work=FIFA.com |publisher=Fédération Internationale de Football Association |access-date=December 2, 2020}}</ref>

After competing in the [[1987 South Pacific Games]] and the [[1994 Polynesia Cup]], ASFA became full members of the [[Oceania Football Confederation]] (OFC) and the [[FIFA|Fédération Internationale de Football Association]] (FIFA) allowing them to compete in the [[FIFA World Cup]] qualifiers for the first time.<ref name="FIFA"/> American Samoa registered their first FIFA-recognized international during the [[1998 Polynesia Cup]] in [[Rarotonga]], [[Cook Islands]] when they lost 3–0 to Tonga on September 2.<ref name="Elo"/><ref name="RSSSF"/><ref name="PC1998">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98oc.html |title=Polynesian Cup 1998 |date=March 21, 2004 |first1=Jarek |last1=Owsianski |first2=José Luis |last2=Pierrend |first3=Josef |last3=Bobrowsky |first4=Andre |last4=Zlotkowski |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=October 31, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221031173822/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98oc.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

==== World record for the worst loss in international matches ====
During their [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|first qualifying campaign]] for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup]] in April 2001, American Samoa was involved in a match which set the record for the largest margin of victory in international football when they [[Australia 31–0 American Samoa|lost 31–0]] to [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2001/apr/11/newsstory.sport9 |title=Aussie Rules as Socceroos smash world record again |last=Rookwood |first=Dan |work=The Guardian |date=April 11, 2001 |access-date=November 24, 2011 |archive-date=October 18, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121018083206/http://www.guardian.co.uk/football/2001/apr/11/newsstory.sport9 |url-status=live }}</ref> The outcome of the match led to debates about the format of qualification tournaments, with the Australian manager [[Frank Farina]] and striker [[Archie Thompson]] – who scored 13 times in the match – feeling that preliminary rounds should be introduced to avoid such unbalanced matches, views shared by the international footballing body [[FIFA]]. It eventually led to the introduction of a preliminary round in the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|Oceanian zone qualification]] for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fifa.com/newscentre/news/newsid=538353.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070624084653/http://www.fifa.com/newscentre/news/newsid=538353.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=June 24, 2007 |title=Road to SA 2010 starts in Samoa |date=June 21, 2007 |access-date=August 5, 2009 |work=FIFA.com |publisher=Fédération Internationale de Football Association }}</ref>

=== Stagnation ===
Since joining FIFA, the team has been regarded as one of the world's weakest teams<ref name="DT">{{cite news |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/football/international/8912337/American-Samoa-wins-first-ever-football-match-after-30-straight-defeats-in-17-years.html |title=American Samoa wins first ever football match after 30 straight defeats in 17 years |work=The Daily Telegraph |date=November 23, 2011 |access-date=November 23, 2011 |archive-date=September 25, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170925040028/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/football/international/8912337/American-Samoa-wins-first-ever-football-match-after-30-straight-defeats-in-17-years.html |url-status=live }}</ref> and were, until November 2011, the joint-lowest ranked national team in the [[FIFA World ranking]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fifa.com/worldranking/rankingtable/index.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111007050949/http://www.fifa.com/worldranking/rankingtable/index.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=October 7, 2011 |title=FIFA/Coca-Cola World Ranking |work=FIFA.com |date=November 23, 2011 |access-date=November 24, 2011}}</ref>

On November 23, 2011, American Samoa recorded their second and first FIFA-recognized win when they defeated [[Tonga national football team|Tonga]] 2–1 in the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|first round of qualifiers]] for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup]]. It was also the team's 1st victory after 38 consecutive defeats.<ref name="Elo"/><ref name="DT"/><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.digitalspy.co.uk/odd/news/a352752/american-samoa-football-team-celebrate-first-win-in-18-years.html |title=American Samoa football team celebrate first win in 18 years |last=Lee |first=Ben |work=Digital Spy |date=November 24, 2011 |access-date=November 24, 2011}}</ref>

The team's efforts to qualify for the 2014 FIFA World Cup were chronicled in a 2014 British documentary, ''[[Next Goal Wins (2014 film)|Next Goal Wins]]'', directed by Mike Brett and Steve Jamison.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.screendaily.com/making-next-goal-wins/5066070.article |title=Making Next Goal Wins |work=screendaily.com |first=Wendy |last=Mitchell |date=February 3, 2014 |access-date=January 24, 2021 |archive-date=October 10, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010212226/https://www.screendaily.com/making-next-goal-wins/5066070.article |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-27245733 |title=Next Goal Wins for 'world's worst football team' |date=May 6, 2014 |access-date=May 6, 2014 |first=Kev |last=Geoghegan |work=BBC News |archive-date=March 6, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190306042805/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-27245733 |url-status=live }}</ref> In October 2015, the territory achieved its highest position in the FIFA World Rankings when they reached 164th following back-to-back wins against [[Tonga national football team|Tonga]] and the [[Cook Islands national football team|Cook Islands]] in [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|qualifying]] for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup]].<ref name="Elo"/><ref name="WCQ2018">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/2018q.html |title=World Cup 2018 Qualifying |date=July 5, 2018 |first1=Ian |last1=King |first2=Karel |last2=Stokkermans |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=July 20, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220720114904/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/2018q.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
 
After playing in the [[Football at the 2019 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2019 Pacific Games]], the team went over four years without playing an international match. This hiatus was caused in part by the [[COVID-19 pandemic]] which forced the cancellation of the [[2020 OFC Nations Cup]] and led the team to withdraw from [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|2022 FIFA World Cup qualification]]. American Samoa was removed from the [[FIFA World Rankings]] in July 2023, due to going 5 years without playing an official match. American Samoa finally competed again at the [[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]].<ref name="football wilderness">{{cite web |last1=Nash |first1=Mathew |title=Pacific nations return from football wilderness |date=November 16, 2023 |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/international/pacific-news/502544/pacific-nations-return-from-football-wilderness |publisher=Radio New Zealand |access-date=16 November 2023}}</ref>

====Player pool====
American Samoa has struggled in part because of its small player pool. The territory has a small population, and many potential players move away for education or work or choose to play other sports such as [[baseball]]. Another limiting factor is that international competition regulations often require that players hold the nationality of the country that they represent, which in the case of American Samoa is [[United States nationality law|United States nationality]].<ref name="Pacific Games">{{cite web |last1=Andrew Fa’asau |first1=Asi |title=FFAS local national teams in final preparations for Pacific Games |date=November 3, 2023 |url=https://www.samoanews.com/sports/ffas-local-national-teams-final-preparations-pacific-games#google_vignette |publisher=Samoa News |access-date=14 November 2023}}</ref> Although people born in American Samoa are [[United States nationality law|United States nationals]],<ref>{{USC|8|1101}}, {{USC|8|1401}}, {{USC|8|1408}}.</ref> foreign nationals who immigrate to American Samoa, who comprise about a third of its population,<ref>{{cite web |url=https://data.census.gov/table/DECENNIALAS2010.PBG8 |title=Nativity by place of birth and citizenship status |work=2010 Decennial Census of the Island Areas, American Samoa summary file |publisher=United States Census Bureau}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://data.census.gov/table/DECENNIALDPAS2020.DP2 |title=Selected social characteristics |work=2020 Decennial Census of the Island Areas, American Samoa demographic profile |publisher=United States Census Bureau}}</ref> are ineligible to apply for United States nationality.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.uscis.gov/sites/default/files/document/guides/chapter4.pdf |title=Who is eligible for naturalization? |publisher=United States Citizenship and Immigration Services}}</ref> An option for the team would be to recruit players from the United States, as other teams of Pacific territories have done with their parent countries, such as [[Tahiti national football team|Tahiti]] drawing players from [[Metropolitan France]].<ref name="Pacific Games"/>

==== 2026 FIFA Series ====
The [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|men's competition]] of the 2026 FIFA Series was split into nine different sections hosted across eight countries. In November 2025, FIFA announced that [[Puerto Rico national football team|Puerto Rico]] would host one such section in [[Bayamón, Puerto Rico|Bayamón]], in which [[Guam national football team|Guam]], the [[United States Virgin Islands national soccer team|U.S. Virgin Islands]] and American Samoa would also compete. The [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)#Puerto Rico|Puerto Rican section of the tournament]] was held in a four-match format (semi-finals, third-place match and final) in late March 2026. American Samoa finished fourth and last in the section after losing against the U.S. Virgin Islands and Guam in the semi-final and third-place match, respectively.<ref name="2026 FIFA Series overview">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 3, 2026 |title=FIFA Series 2026: Everything you need to know |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/news/articles/fifa-series-everything-you-need-to-know |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 1, 2026}}</ref><ref name="2026 FIFA Series champions">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 29, 2026 |title=De Leon propels Puerto Rico to title |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/articles/puerto-rico-champions-guam-finish-third |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 1, 2026}}</ref>

==Team image==
[[File:Pago Park Soccer Stadium.png|thumb|[[Pago Park Soccer Stadium]]|223x223px]]

=== Home stadium ===
{{See also|FFAS Tafuna Soccer Stadium}}

American Samoa's home ground is the [[Pago Park Soccer Stadium]], however American Samoa have never played there in their history at senior level.<ref name="Elo" /> The stadium featured in the 2023 film ''[[Next Goal Wins (2023 film)|Next Goal Wins]]'' and was used as the national team's training ground in the build-up to the [[Football at the 2011 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2011 Pacific Games]] and the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – OFC first round|2014 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Brett |first1=Mike |last2=Brodie |first2=Kristian |last3=Jamison |first3=Steve |title=''[[Next Goal Wins (2023 film)|Next Goal Wins]]'' |date=May 9, 2014 |publisher=Icon Productions}}</ref> Matches at the stadium must be concluded before 6:30pm because of a village curfew which limits noise and prevents the installation of floodlights. For this reason, the association began planning to build a new facility in Tafuna in 2023. FIFA president [[Gianni Infantino]] promised the association $5 million for the project during his visit around Oceania in summer of that year.<ref name="State-of-the-art soccer stadium">{{cite web |last1=Andrew Fa’asau |first1=Asi |title=State-of-the-art soccer stadium for the territory to be built with $5 Million in FIFA funding |date=August 2, 2023 |url=https://www.samoanews.com/sports/state-art-soccer-stadium-territory-be-built-5-million-fifa-funding |publisher=Samoa News |access-date=15 November 2023}}</ref>

===Coaching history===

{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Tiwo Kummings]] (2000)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Anthony Langkilde]] (2001)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Tunoa Lui]] (2001–2002)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[Ian Crook]] (2004)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Nathan Mease]] (2007)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[David Brand (footballer)|David Brand]] (2007–2010)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Iofi Lalogafuafua]] (2011)
*{{flagicon|Netherlands}} [[Thomas Rongen]] (2011)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Larry Mana'o]] (2015–2019)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Tunoa Lui]] (2019–2023)

*{{flagicon|England}} David Jones (2024)
*{{flagicon|Netherlands}} [[Thomas Rongen]] (2024)
*{{flagicon|American Samoa}} [[Shani Simpson]] (2024)
*{{flagicon|Spain}} [[Diego Gómez (footballer, born 1990)|Diego Gómez]] (2026–)
{{div col end}}

== Players ==
===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|2026 FIFA Series]].

''Caps and goals updated as of 9 April 2026, after the game against the [[Guam national football team|Guam]].''

{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs g player|no=6|pos=FW|name=[[Tor Lawrence Mana'o]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|5|27|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=|clubnat=}}
{{nat fs g player|no=10|pos=FW|name=[[Daniel Lee (footballer, born 2007)|Daniel Lee]]|age={{birth date and age|2007|2|17|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=[[Royal Puma]]|clubnat=ASM}}
{{nat fs g player|no=11|pos=FW|name=[[Fiamatai Itamua]]|age={{birth date and age|2007|5|27|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Pago Youth FC]]|clubnat=ASM}}
{{nat fs g player|no=17|pos=FW|name=[[Petu Pouli]]|age={{birth date and age|2005|12|18|df=y}}|caps=8|goals=0|club=[[Vaiala Tonga SC]]|clubnat=ASM}}
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
Goalkeeper [[Nicky Salapu]] holds the record for the most appearances for American Samoa.<ref name="NFT">{{cite web |title=American Samoa |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/4/American_Samoa.html |website=National Football Teams}}</ref> Since his debut against [[Fiji national football team|Fiji]] on April 7, 2001, he has made 24 appearances for the national team – including the world record defeat to Australia and American Samoa's first FIFA-recognized victory against Tonga in November 2011.<ref name="Salapu">{{cite web |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/player/146/Nicky_Salapu.html |title=Nicky Salapu |website=National Football Teams |access-date=May 4, 2021 |archive-date=May 4, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210504173238/https://www.national-football-teams.com/player/146/Nicky_Salapu.html |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Ramin Ott]] holds the record for most goals for American Samoa after scoring three times in 15 appearances between 2004 and 2015.<ref name="Ott">{{cite web |url=http://www.national-football-teams.com/player/7573.html |title=Ramin Ott |website=National Football Teams |access-date=May 4, 2021 |archive-date=March 20, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200320061801/https://www.national-football-teams.com/player/7573/Ramin_Ott.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In total, 10 different players have scored a goal in a FIFA-recognized match for American Samoa.<ref name="NFT"/>

{{Updated|September 9, 2024}}.<ref name="NFT"/>

===Most appearances===

'''Youngest player'''
*[[Jaiyah Saelua]] – 15 years 300 days vs. [[Fiji national football team|Fiji]] on 15 May 2004<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.11v11.com/teams/american-samoa/tab/stats/option/youngest/|title=American Samoa national football team statistics and records: youngest players|accessdate=13 July 2021|publisher=11v11.com|archive-date=October 24, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221024165424/https://www.11v11.com/teams/american-samoa/tab/stats/option/youngest/|url-status=live}}</ref>

'''Oldest player'''
*[[Nicky Salapu]] – 43 years 359 days vs. [[Samoa national football team|Samoa]] on 6 September 2024

'''First goal'''
*[[Duane Atuelevao]] – 12 March 2002 vs. [[Tonga national football team|Tonga]]

'''Most goals in a match'''
*Demetrius Beauchamp – 2 vs. [[Samoa national football team|Samoa]] on 31 August 2015<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.oceaniafootball.com/ofc/News/ViewArticle/tabid/125/Article/e0e0b384-3c6c-40a8-8e82-a5598e321a5c/language/en-US/Default.aspx|title=Samoa 3–2 American Samoa|date=31 August 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150904101134/http://www.oceaniafootball.com/ofc/News/ViewArticle/tabid/125/Article/e0e0b384-3c6c-40a8-8e82-a5598e321a5c/language/en-US/Default.aspx|archive-date=4 September 2015}}</ref>
*Ali'i Mitchell – 2 vs. [[United States Virgin Islands national soccer team|United States Virgin Islands]] on 25 March 2026

==Team records==
*Biggest victory: 3–0 vs. [[Wallis and Futuna national football team|Wallis and Futuna]] on 22 August 1983
*Heaviest defeat: 0–31 vs. [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] on 11 April 2001<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fourfourtwo.com/features/australia-31-0-american-samoa-the-loneliest-game-in-history|title=Australia 31–0 American Samoa: The loneliest game in history|date=9 April 2021|publisher=FourFourTwo|access-date=October 24, 2022|archive-date=October 24, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221024165420/https://www.fourfourtwo.com/features/australia-31-0-american-samoa-the-loneliest-game-in-history|url-status=live}}</ref>
*Most consecutive victories: 2; achieved September 2015
*Most consecutive matches without defeats: 2; achieved November 2011 and September 2015
*Most consecutive matches without victory: 37 between 24 August 1983 and 5 September 2011
*Most consecutive matches without scoring: 7 between 27 August 2007 and 5 September 2011

==Competitive record==
===FIFA World Cup===
Following FIFA affiliation in 1998,<ref name="FIFA"/> American Samoa first entered the qualifying competition for the 2002 FIFA World Cup. They have entered the qualifiers for each subsequent edition but have never made it beyond the first stage. The closest they came to reaching the second phase was in qualifying for the 2018 edition when they won two of their three matches and narrowly missed out on progressing on goal difference.<ref name="WCQ2018"/>

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:100%;"
|-
!colspan="9"|[[FIFA World Cup]]
!rowspan="13"|
!colspan="8"|[[FIFA World Cup qualification|Qualification]]

!Host
!Round

!{{Tooltip|L|Games lost}}
!{{Tooltip|F|Goals scored}}
!{{Tooltip|A|Goals conceded}}
!Source

;Notes
{{Cnote2 Begin|liststyle=disc}}
{{Cnote2|A|'''A:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2006 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2004 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|B|'''B:''' The men's football tournament at the 2007 South Pacific Games doubled as qualifiers for the 2008 OFC Nations Cup and the first round of qualifiers for the 2010 FIFA World Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|C|'''C:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2014 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2012 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|D|'''D:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2018 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2016 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2 End}}

===OFC Nations Cup===
Before becoming full members of OFC in 1998,<ref name="FIFA"/> American Samoa entered the [[1994 Polynesia Cup]] which acted as the qualifying round for Polynesian national teams for the [[1996 OFC Nations Cup]]. This was the territory's first time competing for a place in OFC's flagship competition for senior men's national teams.<ref name="PC1998"/>

The [[OFC Men's Nations Cup]] has often acted as part of the FIFA World Cup qualification process for Oceanian national teams and, as a result, American Samoa have competed in the qualifiers for both competitions in every edition since 1998. They have yet to qualify for either competition.

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:100%;"
|-
!colspan="9"|[[OFC Men's Nations Cup|Oceania Cup / OFC Nations Cup]]
!rowspan="20"|

|align=left|{{flag|New Caledonia|1853}}

|[[1996 OFC Nations Cup|1996]]||{{n/a}}{{Cref2|E}}||colspan=7 rowspan=8{{n/a|''Did not qualify''}}||[[1994 Polynesia Cup|4th]]{{Cref2|F}}||3||0||0||3||3||7||<ref name="PC1994">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/polynesia94.html |title=Polynesian Cup 1994 |date=October 20, 2001 |first=Neil |last=Morrison |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=September 30, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220930225013/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/polynesia94.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

|}

;Notes
{{Cnote2 Begin|liststyle=disc}}
{{Cnote2|E|'''E:''' There was no fixed host for the 1996 OFC Nations Cup or the 2008 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|F|'''F:''' The 1994 Polynesia Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 1996 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|G|'''G:''' The 1998 Polynesia Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 1998 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|H|'''H:''' The 2000 Polynesia Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2000 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|I|'''I:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2006 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2004 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|J|'''J:''' The men's football tournament at the 2007 South Pacific Games doubled as qualifiers for the 2008 OFC Nations Cup and the first round of qualifiers for the 2010 FIFA World Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|K|'''K:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2014 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2012 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|L|'''L:''' The Oceanian qualifiers for the 2018 FIFA World Cup doubled as qualifiers for the 2016 OFC Nations Cup.}}
{{Cnote2 End}}

===Pacific Games===
American Samoa first entered the South Pacific Games in 1983. This was the first time a team representing the territory had competed in association football and they recorded one win from their three games in the group stage by beating Wallis and Futuna 3–0 – the only time American Samoa have recorded a win in the competition – but that wasn't enough to progress to the next round.<ref name="SPG1983"/> American Samoa would go onto enter the 1987 edition where they were again eliminated in the group stage, losing all four of their matches.<ref name="SPG1987">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac87.html |title=South Pacific Games 1987 (New Caledonia) |date=January 19, 2017 |first=Erik |last=Garin |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=October 31, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221031173813/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac87.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

It would be another 20 years before they again entered the competition. The 2007 edition was the last known as the South Pacific Games before becoming the Pacific Games four years later and it was also part of the qualification process for the 2010 FIFA World Cup. In their third appearance in the competition, American Samoa were again eliminated at the group stage, losing all four matches.<ref name="SPG2007"/> The same fate befell American Samoa in the 2011 Pacific Games as they lost all five games to finish bottom of their group.<ref name="PG2011">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pacgames2011.html |title=Pacific Games 2011 (New Caledonia) |date=January 18, 2018 |first1=Neil |last1=Morrison |first2=Hans |last2=Schöggl |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=July 29, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220729210312/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pacgames2011.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

In 2015, the Pacific Games was an age-restricted tournament that doubled as the [[OFC Men's Olympic Qualifying Tournament]] and no team representing American Samoa took part. Four years later, they were again eliminated at the group stage however, a 1–1 draw with [[Tuvalu national football team|Tuvalu]] ended a 32-year losing streak in the competition.<ref name="PG2019">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pacgames2019.html |title=Pacific Games 2019 (Samoa) |date=August 21, 2019 |first=Roberto |last=Di Maggio |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=December 2, 2020 |archive-date=August 3, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220803044607/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pacgames2019.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:100%;"
!colspan=10|[[Pacific Games]]
|-
!Year
!Host
!Round
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Games won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Games drawn, including knockout matches decided on penalty kicks}}
!{{Tooltip|L|Games lost}}
!{{Tooltip|F|Goals scored}}
!{{Tooltip|A|Goals conceded}}
!Source

;Notes
{{Cnote2 Begin|liststyle=disc}}
{{Cnote2|M|'''M:''' The men's football tournament at the 2007 South Pacific Games doubled as qualifiers for the 2008 OFC Nations Cup and the first round of qualifiers for the 2010 FIFA World Cup.}}
{{Cnote2|N|'''N:''' The men's football tournament at the 2015 Pacific Games was an age-restricted competition that doubled as the OFC Men's Olympic Qualifying Tournament.}}
{{Cnote2 End}}

==Records and statistics==
{{Main|American Samoa national football team results}}

American Samoa played their first full international match against [[Papua New Guinea national football team|Papua New Guinea]], which ended in a 20–0 loss. Their national team have suffered the [[Australia 31–0 American Samoa|world's biggest international defeat]], by losing to [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] 31–0. American Samoa have won only against [[Cook Islands national football team|Cook Islands]], [[Tonga national football team|Tonga]], and [[Wallis and Futuna national football team|Wallis and Futuna]]. American Samoa have also lost to all of these teams at least once.

===Head-to-head record===
The following table shows the American Samoa national football team's all-time international record. The statistics are composed of [[FIFA World Cup]], [[OFC Nations Cup]], [[Polynesia Cup]] and [[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]] matches, as well as international friendlies.
{{#lst:American Samoa national football team results|Record by opponent}}

===Performance by competition===
{{#invoke:sports table|main|style=WDL
|show_positions=n
|ranking_style=wperc
|sortable_table=yes
|team_header=Competition

|team1=WCQ|name_WCQ=[[FIFA World Cup qualification]]{{Cref2|N}}
|team2=ONC|name_ONC=[[OFC Nations Cup|OFC Nations Cup qualification]]{{Cref2|N}}
|team3=PNC|name_PNC=[[Polynesia Cup]]{{Cref2|N}}
|team4=PAG|name_PAG=[[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]{{Cref2|N}}

|win_WCQ=3|draw_WCQ=1|loss_WCQ=15|gf_WCQ=11|ga_WCQ=138
|win_ONC=3|draw_ONC=1|loss_ONC=25|gf_ONC=21|ga_ONC=167
|win_PNC=0|draw_PNC=0|loss_PNC=11|gf_PNC=8 |ga_PNC=59
|win_PAG=1|draw_PAG=1|loss_PAG=23|gf_PAG=12|ga_PAG=179

|update=complete|source=<ref name="matches">{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/amsamoa-intres.html|website=[[RSSSF]]|title=American Samoa – List of International Matches|access-date=February 2, 2023|archive-date=February 28, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230228012805/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/amsamoa-intres.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="NFT"/>
}}

{{#invoke:sports table|main|style=WDL
|show_positions=n
|ranking_style=wperc
|show_totals=yes
|sortable_table=yes
|team_header=Competition

|team1=COM|name_COM=Competitive
|team2=FRI|name_FRI=[[Exhibition game#Association football|Friendlies]]

|win_COM=4|draw_COM=2|loss_COM=49|gf_COM=32|ga_COM=367
|win_FRI=1|draw_FRI=0|loss_FRI=1 |gf_FRI=2 |ga_FRI=7

|update=complete|source=<ref name="matches"/><ref name="NFT"/>
}}

;Notes
{{Cnote2 Begin|liststyle=disc}}
{{Cnote2|N|'''N:''' A number of matches in the Polynesia Cup and the Pacific Games doubled as qualifiers for the ONC Nations Cup and the FIFA World Cup. For full details, see [[American Samoa national football team results#Results|American Samoa national football team results]].}}
{{Cnote2 End}}

}}
